guideline_references: NICE NG206: ME/CFS diagnosis and management (2021)
IOM 2015 diagnostic criteria for ME/CFS (systemic exertion intolerance disease)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute sore throat / pharyngitis (current)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: an alarming acute presentation (severe sore throat, huge tender neck glands, inability to swallow fluids, profound collapse) plus an explicit 'urgent, rule out peritonsillar abscess/airway compromise' framing sits on top of a benign, recognised, recurrent post-viral fatigue flare. The source's own acute red-flag screen is negative (airway patent, no drooling/stridor, able to swallow saliva, whisper-not-muffled voice, stable vitals) and its disposition is home management + telehealth GP review in days. The correct telehealth answer is to recognise the recurrent flare, tier T2 (prompt GP review), and attach prominent MANDATORY airway + hydration safety-netting — NOT to blanket-escalate to ED/000.

Telehealth reprojection: the in-home clinician's oropharyngeal examination, lymph-node palpation and measured vitals (which cleared the airway and excluded quinsy) are sealed in nodes 10/11 with telehealth_limits — the AI Doctor cannot reproduce them, so the unexcludable throat pathology (quinsy/epiglottitis) and dehydration must be handled as conservative safety-netting, not baseline emergency escalation.

Commission traps: prescribing antibiotics for a functional/viral immune flare with no bacterial evidence (source explicitly states no antibiotics indicated); recommending an NSAID without accounting for the dehydration/renal risk.
Age (~24) and sex (female) inferred from epidemiology + 'patient and parent present' / parent-dependent context — NOT stated in the source. Flagged for clinician confirmation.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
